mechanism_narrative: Rising retail sales (RSAFS) signal strengthening consumer demand and corporate earnings growth, driving positive S&P 500 returns over the next 20 trading days. The mechanism operates through improved profit expectations and reduced recession risk.
evidence_refs: fact:retail sales at 339.3B in March 2005 indicate sustained consumer spending
failure_condition: Rank IC < 0.03 over any 2-year rolling window, or positive RSAFS momentum fails to produce positive 20d returns in > 55% of triggered instances.

mechanism_narrative: When the federal funds rate exceeds 2.5% and continues rising, the Fed is in active tightening mode. This signals future economic slowdown and equity multiple compression, producing negative S&P 500 returns over the next 60 trading days as investors front-run earnings downgrades.
evidence_refs: fact:FEDFUNDS at 2.63% in March 2005 indicates tightening trajectory
failure_condition: SPX produces positive 60d returns in > 60% of instances where FEDFUNDS > 2.5% and rising, or Rank IC < 0.02 over any 3-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: Declining unemployment rate (UNRATE) combined with rising payroll employment (PAYEMS) signals tight labor markets and wage pressure, which increases corporate costs and reduces profit margins. This dual signal predicts negative equity returns over 20 trading days as investors reprice earnings expectations downward.
evidence_refs: fact:UNRATE at 5.2% and PAYEMS at 132.9M in March 2005
failure_condition: The interaction signal fails to produce negative 20d returns in > 55% of triggered instances, or joint Rank IC of UNRATE and PAYEMS < 0.02 over any 2-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: Industrial production (INDPRO) growth accelerates above its 3-month trend, signaling robust supply-side capacity utilization and pricing power. This produces positive S&P 500 returns over 5 trading days as investors reprrice growth expectations upward and reduce recession probability.
evidence_refs: fact:INDPRO at 118.5 in March 2005 reflects sustained industrial activity
failure_condition: Rank IC < 0.04 over any 1-year rolling window, or positive INDPRO momentum fails to produce positive 5d returns in > 52% of instances.

mechanism_narrative: Housing starts (HOUST) decline by more than 10% month-over-month while simultaneously FEDFUNDS remains above 2.5%, signaling a demand shock in the housing sector that will propagate to construction employment and consumer wealth. This produces negative S&P 500 returns over 60 trading days.
evidence_refs: fact:HOUST at 2195k in February 2005 with rising rates
regime_hint: monetary tightening with housing demand shock
failure_condition: The joint signal fails to predict negative 60d returns in > 58% of triggered instances, or interaction Rank IC < 0.02 over any 3-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: Inflation (CPIAUCSL) accelerates above 2.5% year-over-year while FEDFUNDS lags behind inflation, creating negative real rates. This signals monetary accommodation that supports equity valuations through lower real discount rates, producing positive S&P 500 returns over 20 trading days.
candidate_trigger: CPIAUCSL growth > 2.5% annualized and FEDFUNDS < CPIAUCSL growth rate
evidence_refs: fact:CPIAUCSL at 192.0 in February 2005 with FEDFUNDS at 2.63%
regime_hint: accommodative monetary policy with rising inflation
failure_condition: Negative real rate signal fails to produce positive 20d returns in > 58% of triggered instances, or interaction Rank IC < 0.02 over any 2-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: Real GDP growth (GDPC1) decelerates to below 2% annualized rate, signaling late-cycle slowdown and reduced corporate earnings growth. This produces negative S&P 500 returns over 60 trading days as investors reduce equity risk exposure in anticipation of recession.
evidence_refs: fact:GDPC1 at 10994.3B in Q4 2004 reflects economic momentum
failure_condition: Sub-2% GDP growth fails to produce negative 60d returns in > 56% of instances, or Rank IC < 0.02 over any 3-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: Unemployment rate (UNRATE) rises by more than 0.3 percentage points over a 3-month period, signaling labor market deterioration and reduced consumer confidence. This produces negative S&P 500 returns over 20 trading days as investors reprice earnings and reduce equity allocations.
evidence_refs: fact:UNRATE at 5.2% in March 2005 provides baseline for deterioration detection
failure_condition: Rising unemployment fails to produce negative 20d returns in > 55% of triggered instances, or Rank IC < 0.03 over any 2-year rolling window.
